cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Okay, here's one that trips people up in real interviews.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Your suite runs, createUser fails, and right below it deleteUser shows up as skipped, not failed, and your teammate insists that's a bug in TestNG because a broken flow should produce 2 failures, not 1 failure and a skip.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: So the interviewer looks at you and asks, is TestNG broken here, or is it doing exactly what it was designed to do?

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: And if your gut said that's a bug, well, this is the episode for you.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: I'm Leo, I spend my days writing automation and my nights getting grilled about it in mock interviews.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And I'm Maya, staff SDET, former hiring manager, and today we're covering TestNG, the Java test framework that shows up in almost every Selenium-flavored interview loop.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And here's the thing, TestNG isn't just a Java detail, because almost every test runner on the planet shares the same skeleton, lifecycle hooks, assertions, data-driven inputs, grouping, parallelism, and reporting.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: So if you truly understand TestNG, you can read JUnit 5, NUnit, and pytest at a glance, which means this 1 topic quietly covers 4.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: That's actually why interviewers love it, right, it's a proxy question.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: They ask about TestNG but what they're really probing is whether you understand how test runners work at all.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Exactly, and the name itself is a hint, the NG stands for Next Generation, because it was built to go beyond plain unit testing with flexible configuration, data-driven tests, groups, dependencies, and built-in parallelism, which is why it became the default runner in most Java plus Selenium stacks.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: So let's build it up in 3 chunks, starting with the lifecycle.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: The annotations, this is the one they ask in the first 5 minutes of every screen.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Right, and the mental model I want you to carry is a concert venue.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Think about what happens on show night.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: The venue itself gets unlocked and powered up once for the whole evening, then the stage gets set for a particular act, then each band does its own setup, and then before every single song the crew resets the mics and the lighting.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: TestNG is exactly that, nested rings of setup from the widest scope down to the narrowest.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: At-BeforeSuite and at-AfterSuite run once around the entire suite, that's unlocking the venue.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: At-BeforeTest and at-AfterTest wrap a test tag inside the testng.xml file, that's setting the stage for one act.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: At-BeforeClass and at-AfterClass run once per test class, that's the band's own soundcheck.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And at-BeforeMethod and at-AfterMethod run before and after every single at-Test method, that's the reset between songs.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: So the order going in is suite, test, class, method, and then when everything tears down, the after hooks unwind in reverse, method first, suite last, like walking back out of the building the way you came in.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: That's the answer word for word, and notice how natural it is once you have the venue picture, because you'd never lock the whole building before the band finished packing up.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: In a Selenium context the classic pattern is at-BeforeMethod creates a fresh driver, the at-Test does its thing, and at-AfterMethod quits the driver, so every test gets a clean browser.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: And the translation layer, since you promised this covers 4 frameworks.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: At-BeforeMethod is JUnit 5's at-BeforeEach, at-BeforeClass is roughly at-BeforeAll, NUnit calls them SetUp and OneTimeSetUp, and pytest does the same job with fixtures scoped to function, class, or session.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Same lifecycle, different spelling.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Perfect.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Chunk 2 is assertions and data-driven testing, and these are 2 of the most reliable interview questions in existence.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: First, hard versus soft assertions.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: A hard assertion, your standard Assert dot assertEquals, fails fast, meaning the test stops dead at the first failed check.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: A soft assertion, using the SoftAssert class, collects every failure quietly and only reports them all together when you call assertAll at the end.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Give me the analogy, because I always blank on which is which under pressure.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Think of a home inspector.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: The hard-assert inspector walks into the house, spots a crack in the foundation, turns around, and leaves, so you get a report with exactly 1 problem and no idea what else is wrong.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: The soft-assert inspector walks the entire house, notes the crack, the leaky faucet, the bad wiring, and hands you 1 complete report at the end.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: That final handover, that moment of handing you the full report, is the assertAll call, and here's the detail candidates miss, if you forget to call assertAll, nothing ever fails, the inspector just never files the report, and your broken test passes silently.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Ooh, that's a nasty one.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: So the memory hook is, soft assert without assertAll is an inspector who never mails the report.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: And when would I actually choose soft asserts?

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: When you're validating several independent things in 1 test, say 5 fields on a profile page, and you want to see all the broken ones in a single run instead of playing whack-a-mole 1 failure at a time.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And the mapping, JUnit 5 has an assertAll method, NUnit has Assert dot Multiple, and AssertJ and Playwright both ship soft assertions, so the concept travels everywhere.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Now, staying in this chunk, data-driven tests, because it's the same theme of 1 test body, many checks.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: TestNG gives you 2 doors.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Door 1 is at-DataProvider, a method that returns a 2-dimensional Object array, and TestNG runs your test once per row, so if your provider returns a valid login row and an invalid login row, the same test method executes twice with different inputs.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Door 2 is at-Parameters, which injects simple values from testng.xml, things like a browser name or a base URL.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: So the way I'd keep those straight is, DataProvider is for the actual test data, the rows, the interesting combinations, stuff that lives in code or gets computed, while Parameters is for environment-ish knobs that ops or CI might want to flip without recompiling anything.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: That's exactly the split, and if the interviewer pushes on other frameworks, JUnit 5 does this with at-ParameterizedTest plus sources, NUnit uses TestCase attributes, and pytest uses parametrize.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Okay, chunk 3, and this is where TestNG really earns its keep in interviews, groups, dependencies, and parallel execution, all orchestrated through testng.xml.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And my analogy for testng.xml is an air traffic control tower.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: The planes are your test classes, and the code inside them doesn't know or care about scheduling, but the tower decides everything about the traffic, which flights take off tonight, meaning which classes and groups get selected, what instructions each pilot receives, meaning the parameters injected, and critically, how many runways operate at once, meaning the parallel mode and the thread count.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And the beautiful part is that the tower changes the schedule without rebuilding any planes, you reconfigure the whole suite without recompiling code.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: So when someone asks what testng.xml is, the answer is, it's the suite descriptor, it selects classes and groups, passes parameters, and configures parallelism, all without touching or recompiling the tests themselves.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Nailed it.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Now the pieces the tower controls.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Groups first, you tag a test with at-Test groups equals smoke, and then from testng.xml or the command line you run just that group, which is how teams carve a fast smoke slice out of a big regression suite.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: That's the same idea as JUnit 5 tags, NUnit categories, and pytest markers.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Then priority, at-Test priority equals 1, which simply orders independent tests, lower numbers run first and the default is 0.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And then dependencies, dependsOnMethods and dependsOnGroups, which say this test only makes sense if that one passed, and if the prerequisite fails, the dependent test gets skipped rather than failed.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Which brings us right back to your cold open.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: CreateUser fails, deleteUser gets skipped, and that is not a bug, that's the whole point, because deleteUser failing would be a misleading second failure, there was never a user to delete, so the skip is TestNG telling you the truth, only 1 thing is actually broken.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Exactly right, and that sets up trap corner, because there are 2 classic wrong answers buried in this area.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Trap number 1 is confusing priority with dependsOnMethods.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Under pressure, candidates say they both just control test order, and that answer costs points, because priority is cosmetic ordering of independent tests, nothing more, while dependsOnMethods creates a real prerequisite relationship that can skip dependents when the prerequisite fails.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: One is a seating chart, the other is a load-bearing wall.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: And trap number 2, I'm guessing, is the over-eager candidate who hears all this and starts bragging about their beautiful 10-test dependency chain where login feeds into search which feeds into checkout.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: That's the big one, and it's sneaky because it sounds like sophistication.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Heavy use of dependsOnMethods couples your tests together, so 1 upstream failure cascades into a wall of skips, your report gets murky, and worse, chained tests can't run in parallel, because parallelism assumes tests are independent.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: So the strong answer is, I know dependencies exist, I use them sparingly for genuine setup ordering, and I prefer independent, isolated tests, and you can even add that JUnit deliberately limits ordering features for exactly this reason.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Saying that sentence tells the interviewer you've maintained a real suite, not just read the docs.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: So dodge the trap by naming the trade-off before they do.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: And that flows straight into parallelism, since you just said independence is what unlocks it.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Right, so in testng.xml you set parallel to methods, tests, classes, or instances, plus a thread-count, say 4, and TestNG fans your suite out across threads.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: But, and this is the follow-up interviewers always spring, parallel tests must be thread-safe, they can share no mutable state, and in Selenium specifically the classic answer is a ThreadLocal wrapper around the WebDriver, so each thread gets its very own driver instead of 4 threads fighting over 1 browser.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Back to the airport, 4 runways only work because each plane has its own pilot, its own fuel, its own instruments, and the moment 2 flights share a pilot the whole schedule collapses.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: ThreadLocal means every thread gets its own car keys, nobody shares a steering wheel.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Got it.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: What about the last mile, reporting and the extension points?

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Quick tour, because it comes up as a how-would-you question.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Listeners hook into the test lifecycle, so implementing ITestListener and overriding onTestFailure is the canonical way to capture a screenshot on failure and attach it to the report, and you register the listener with the at-Listeners annotation or in testng.xml.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: For flaky tests there's IRetryAnalyzer, which re-runs a failed test a set number of times, and the senior move is to immediately add that retries should be used sparingly because they mask real flakiness rather than fixing it.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And for reporting, TestNG emits HTML and XML out of the box, but real teams usually layer Allure or ExtentReports on top for richer output.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Same extension idea exists everywhere, JUnit 5 has TestWatcher, pytest has hooks in conftest.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Alright, time for the in-the-room segment.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: How does this actually surface when I'm sitting across from the panel?

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Three flavors.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: The screener asks definitional stuff, annotation order, hard versus soft assert, what testng.xml is, and those need crisp 1-breath answers.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: The mid-round asks design questions, how would you run only smoke tests, how would you get a 4-hour regression suite under 30 minutes, and the shape of that answer is groups for selection plus parallel with thread-count plus ThreadLocal for safety.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: And the senior round probes judgment, they'll invite you to praise dependencies or retries and watch whether you volunteer the downsides, so the meta-skill is pairing every feature with its cost.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Okay, 60-second recap, here we go.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: If they ask the annotation order, say suite, test, class, method going in, at-Test in the middle, and the after hooks unwind in reverse, like leaving the concert venue the way you came in.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: If they ask hard versus soft assertions, say hard fails fast at the first broken check, soft collects everything and reports at assertAll, and mention that forgetting assertAll means failures vanish, the inspector who never mails the report.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: And if they ask why TestNG over JUnit, say same core ideas, but TestNG adds the testng.xml suite descriptor, DataProvider, groups, and dependencies, which shine for integration and end-to-end suites, then earn the senior nod by adding that you keep tests independent so parallelism stays cheap and failures stay honest.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: That's the episode.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Skips are a feature, dependency chains are a smell, and 1 framework's skeleton unlocks 4.

cues.speaker: MAYA
cues.text: Next time you're studying, take the comparison table, TestNG, JUnit 5, NUnit, pytest, and quiz yourself dialect to dialect, because that cross-mapping is what makes you sound fluent instead of memorized.

cues.speaker: LEO
cues.text: Keep your tests independent and your assertions honest, and we'll see you in the next one.
